title: Euro-Style Kitchen Sink Base Cabinet
summary: A frameless sink base cabinet built from precisely-ripped plywood, sized for a compact kitchen and hung with concealed euro hinges.
description:
Frameless ("euro-style") cabinetry drops the face frame entirely, which means the plywood itself is the finished surface — and that raises the bar on precision. The plan calls out that the rip width on your plywood strips must be EXACTLY consistent, in capital letters, and that's not overcaution: with no face frame to hide a discrepancy, an inconsistent rip shows up as a visible step where two cabinets meet.

Edge banding goes on before assembly, not after, ironed onto every exposed plywood edge on the sides and bottom while the pieces are still flat and easy to work — trying to iron banding onto an assembled cabinet's inside corners is a losing fight. The center support does double duty tying the front support to the bottom, which is what keeps a sink base's typically wider, unsupported front opening from racking under the weight of a full sink and countertop. Because it's frameless, this cabinet needs 1/2" overlay concealed euro hinges specifically — not the standard 1-1/4" overlay hinges used on face-frame cabinets — since the mounting geometry is different.

tools.note: Rip width must be exactly consistent across every plywood strip — a frameless cabinet has no face frame to hide a discrepancy.
Iron banding onto every exposed plywood edge before assembly, while pieces are still flat.

materials.name: Plywood, 3/4", cabinet grade or hardwood, sheet
materials.note: Rip to exactly 23" wide strips — consistency matters more than usual on a frameless build.

materials.name: Pine, 1x4, 8 ft
materials.note: Top, back, front, and center supports.

materials.name: Pine, 1x2, 8 ft
materials.note: Species-matched to your doors if staining, or paint-grade for painted doors.

materials.name: Concealed euro hinges, 1/2" overlay frameless
materials.note: Not interchangeable with standard face-frame overlay hinges — mounting geometry differs.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Rip the 3/4" plywood to EXACTLY 23" wide and keep the fence untouched for every strip — on a frameless cabinet there is no face frame to hide a rip that wanders, and the error shows as a visible step where two cabinets meet. From those strips cut two sides 23" x 29 1/4" and one bottom 23" x 34 1/2". From the two 8 ft 1x4s cut three supports at 34 1/2" and one front support at 24 3/4" — two 34 1/2" pieces plus the 24 3/4" piece fit on the first board and the third 34 1/2" piece comes off the second, which is why two boards are listed. Cut the 1/4" back panel 36" x 29 1/4". The 2x6 for the toekick build-up and the 1x2 have no lengths in the cut list, so leave them until the box is assembled and cut them to what you measure. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw.
materials: Plywood, 3/4", cabinet grade or hardwood, sheet

steps.title: Edge-band the exposed edges
steps.body: Iron edge banding onto every plywood edge that will show at the front of the finished cabinet — the front edge of both sides and the front edge of the bottom — while the pieces are still flat on the bench. Run the iron slowly enough to melt the adhesive through, press the banding down with a block as it cools, then trim it flush and sand the corner just enough to break the arris without cutting through the veneer. Doing this before assembly is the whole point: once the box is together, the inside corners are a losing fight for an iron. Skip it only if the cabinet is getting painted.
materials: Plywood, 3/4", cabinet grade or hardwood, sheet

steps.title: Attach the bottom to the sides
steps.body: The two 29 1/4" sides run the full height of the cabinet and the 34 1/2" bottom fits between them, which makes the finished box 36" wide overall — the same 36" as the back panel, and the standard width for a sink base. Drill 3/4"-setting pocket holes along both ends and the underside edges of the bottom panel, then clamp it between the sides and drive 1 1/4" pocket screws with glue in the joint. Keep the bottom's front edge flush with the sides' front edges. Check both diagonals across the U you have just built before the glue grabs; every part that follows registers off this assembly.
materials: Plywood, 3/4", cabinet grade or hardwood, sheet

steps.title: Attach the top supports
steps.body: Two of the three 34 1/2" 1x4 supports go across the top of the box — one at the front edge and one at the back — fitting between the sides exactly as the bottom does. Drill their pocket holes so the holes face UP: nothing sits above them, so they are hidden by the countertop and you still get the stronger end-grain-free joint. Fasten with 1 1/4" pocket screws and glue, keeping the top edges flush with the tops of the sides so the countertop lands on a flat plane. Recheck the box for square across the top once both are in.

steps.title: Add the back and center supports
steps.body: The third 34 1/2" support is the back support, fitted between the sides at the top rear and tied into the top support as well — this is the part the cabinet hangs from once it is screwed to the wall studs, so it takes glue and pocket screws into both the sides and the adjoining top support. Then fit the front support and a center support that ties it down to the bottom panel; that pair is what stops a sink base's unusually wide, unsupported front opening from racking under a loaded countertop and a full sink. The cut list has no row for the center support and gives the front support as 24 3/4", which is not the cabinet's 34 1/2" clear width, so measure the actual gap in your assembled box and cut both to fit rather than to the list. Fasten everything with 1 1/4" pocket screws and glue.

steps.title: Attach the back panel
steps.body: The 36" x 29 1/4" back panel is the exact outside footprint of the assembled box, so it is also your final squaring tool. Start from two factory edges of the plywood, line one corner up with a corner of the cabinet, and fasten that edge first with glue and 3/4" staples or brads; then pull the opposite corner into line with the panel's edge before nailing it. Nail into the sides, the bottom and the back support all the way around, roughly every 4" to 6". Once this panel is on square, the cabinet cannot rack.

steps.title: Hang the doors
steps.body: Measure your actual opening rather than working from the list, then size the two doors so that together they cover the full 36" outside width of the box with a consistent gap between them — a frameless cabinet has no frame to hide behind, so the doors ARE the front. Use 1/2" overlay concealed euro hinges rated for frameless cabinets; standard face-frame overlay hinges bore and mount to a different geometry and will not sit right on plywood edges. Bore the cup holes in the doors, screw the mounting plates to the inside faces of the sides, hang the doors, and then use the hinges' own adjustment screws to bring the gap and the door faces into line. Adjust both doors against each other, not one at a time, so the reveal down the middle stays even.

steps.title: Fit the toekick and remaining trim
steps.body: With the box assembled, measure and cut the 2x6 toekick and any 1x2 trim to the actual front opening and side lengths rather than to a canned figure — that is why these two boards have no cut-list rows. Fasten the toekick under the front of the carcass with pocket holes or screws from inside, and pin the 1x2 trim where your layout needs an edge cover.

images.alt: Frameless euro-style kitchen sink base cabinet with concealed hinges
